note:
当我年轻而破产时，背着本科/研究生贷款，我住在曼哈顿-一个我付不起的昂贵城市。我在第3街附近的第17街的第一间公寓是三层楼的向上走工作室，每月租金$ 400。我搬进了位于Lexington Avenue和27th street的两居室，价格为1100美元/月，带有一个小厨房，一个浴室，然后与2-3个人共享。它有一个很大的露台和景色，但没有空间或隐私。
 没关系：年轻，长生不老，玩乐时，您可以过着“租穷”的生活方式，在享受城市生活的同时还可以讨价还价。实际上，曼哈顿提供了无休止的免费（或廉价）娱乐，聚会，社交活动。用餐很便宜，有种族，而且总是“便宜而开朗”。您可以用很少的钱来赚钱。
 当您破产时，您会敏锐地讨价还价。您的时间比金钱还多，因此，如果它节省了现金，您就不必担心排队。在Strand找书，在The Gap找便宜货的架子，在Sample Sales找东西，在Century 21买到去年的冬装，在Rothmans买到便宜的西服。
  这种生活方式有一笔未实现的成本。当您破产时，会有一种无法实现愿望的全部潜在心理。仅仅因为您无法拥有，就可能会想要拥有您无法拥有的东西。当你贫穷时，你几乎不可能拥有一切。这导致可疑的决策。
 在壁橱上尝试使用这种组织技巧：收拾好所有衣服并将它们向后悬挂-吧台上的衣架钩的开口朝向而不是远离您。穿些东西，将钩子恢复正常。等一年您所穿的衣服钩在背对您的钩子上，而您一年未穿的衣服正对着您。
 我这样做是为了取得令人震惊和有益的结果。令人震惊的是，因为我意识到我实际上穿的“衣柜”很少。内容丰富，因为我立即意识到我在追逐“便宜货”上浪费了多少钱，这些东西我不一定需要甚至不需要，而是已经出售了。这是我壁橱的75％。
  我拒绝先考虑价格，而不是先考虑价格，至少要等到结账时才考虑价格，而不是先考虑商店价格，从而让零售商的花样影响我的决策。如果我仍然想要XX美元的东西，那我一定很想要，所以我就买了。如果我认为它相对于质量和我的需求而言太昂贵了，我不会。
1.我少买东西。我的交易量比以前便宜很多。似乎有东西要出售的事实与我的演算无关。
 2.我只会买更好的质量。不再胡扯，不再有出口“ B”商品，也没有停产。我真正需要和/或真正想要的只有非常优质的物品。
 3.我限制我拥有的东西。我只买我真正想要的东西。对于每一个新进来的东西，旧的东西都会掉出来–经常（令人震惊地）未使用并捐赠给当地的旧货店。
 4.我节省了精神上的带宽。我不会浪费时间和精力进行讨价还价。它释放了大脑的周期，可以进行创造性的工作。
 5.我节省时间。我不喜欢黑色星期五，也不喜欢网络星期一。如果我的愿望清单上有东西，并且Amazon Prime Day给我发送了电子邮件，我会考虑进行购买。但是我发现，将某物放到愿望清单上的行为几乎与购买它的空洞手势一样令人满意。
 我从年轻的时候开始进行这项实验，并且收入有限，现在我已经老了，并且收入较少，并且继续了这种方法。
 这是令人难以置信的释放。破产最具有挑战性的部分是精神上的带宽-我发现观看每一分钱在心理上都精疲力尽，而当我最终只是停止讨价还价时，我就充分利用了这种能力。每当我退缩时，我几乎都会后悔。
这不是关于唯物主义弊端的摇摆不清的演讲；我不在紧急情况下；我没有小房子。我只是选择将自己的钱花在我真正想要的东西上，而不是陷入讨价还价者的困境。
note_en:
When I was young and broke, carrying undergrad/grad school loans, I lived in Manhattan — an expensive city I could barely afford. My first apartment 17th street near 3rd Avenue was a three floor walk up studio at $400 per month. I moved into a two bedroom at 90 Lexington Avenue & 27th street — $1100/mo, with a tiny galley kitchen, one bathroom, and I shared it with 2-3 other people. It had a great terrace and view, but no space or privacy.
 It didn’t matter: When you are young, immortal, and having fun, you can live a great “rent poor” lifestyle, bargain hunt necessities while enjoying city life. Indeed, Manhattan provided endless free (or cheap) entertainment, parties, socializing; Dining was inexpensive, ethnic and always “ Cheap & cheerful.” You could get by on very little cash.
 When you are broke you develop a keen eye for a bargain. You have more time than money, so you don’t mind lines if it saves cash. Find books at the Strand, haunt bargain racks at The Gap, stuff at Sample Sales, see what last year’s winter coats go for at Century 21, get cheap suits at Rothmans.
  There was an unrealized cost to this lifestyle. When you are broke, there is an entire underlying psychology of unfulfilled desire. It creates a danger of wanting what you cannot have  simply because you cannot have it. And when you are poor, you cannot have nearly everything. This leads to questionable decision-making.
 Try this organization trick for your closet: Take all of your clothes and hang them backwards – the opening of the hanger hook on the bar facing towards, instead of away from you. Wear something, return the hook to the normal. Wait 1 year. The clothes you have worn are on hooks facing away from you, while the clothes you have not worn for a year are facing towards you.
 I did this, to shocking and informative result. Shocking, because I realized how little of my “wardrobe” I actually wore; informative because I instantly realized how much money I had wasted chasing “bargains” — things I did not necessarily need or even want, but rather had been on sale. This was 75% of my closet.
  Rather than shop price first, and thereby allow the retailer’s bag of tricks to influence my decision making, I  refused to even look at prices – at least until I got to checkout. If I still wanted something at XX dollars, then I must  really want it, and so I buy it. If I decide its too expensive for what it is relative to its quality and my wants/needs, I don’t.
  1.  I buy much less stuff. I make far fewer purchases than I was prior in my bargain hunting days. The fact something appears to be on sale is irrelevant to my calculus.
 2.  I only buy better quality. No more crap, no more outlet “B” goods, nothing discontinued. Only very fine quality items I actually need and/or really want.
 3.  I limit what I own. I only buy what I truly want. And for each new item that comes in, something old goes out – often (shockingly) unworn and donated to local thrift shop.
 4.  I save mental bandwidth. I don’t waste time and effort bargain hunting. It frees up brain cycles for mroe creative and fulfilling efforts.
 5.  I save time. I don’t give shit about Black Friday or a care about Cyber Monday. If something is on my wish list and Amazon Prime Day sends me an email, I think about making that purchase. But I find the mere act of putting something on a wish list is nearly as fulfilling as the empty gesture of purchasing it.
 I began this experiment when young(ish) and of limited means and have continued this approach now that I am old(ish) and of less limited means.
 This was incredibly freeing. The most challenging part of being broke was the mental bandwidth — I found it psychologically exhausting to watch every penny, and when I eventually just stopped bargain hunting, I put that capacity to much better use. Anytime I backslid I regretted it almost immediately.
 This is not a finger wagging lecture on the evils of materialism; I am not in the FIRE contingency; I don’t own a tiny home. I just am choosing to spend my dollars on things I really want, rather than falling into the bargain hunter’s dilemma.
title: 讨价还价猎人的困境–大局
title_en: The Bargain Hunter's Dilemma – The Big Picture

note:
威胁执行者目前正在为世界各地数百家公司的C级高管的电子邮件帐户出售密码。
     ZDNet本周获悉，这些数据正在名为Exploit.in的俄语黑客的封闭式地下论坛上出售。
 威胁参与者正在出售Office 365和Microsoft帐户的电子邮件和密码组合，他声称这些组合由拥有以下功能的高级管理人员所拥有：
  根据公司的规模和用户的角色，对这些帐户的访问权价格从100美元到1,500美元不等。
  网络安全社区中的一位消息人士同意与卖方联系以获取样本，并证实了数据的有效性并获得了两个帐户的有效凭证，这两个帐户是美国一家中型软件公司的首席执行官和一家欧盟公司的CFO。零售商店链。
 消息人士要求ZDNet不使用其名称，该消息人士正在通知两家公司，同时还通知了两家其他公司，卖方为其发布了帐户密码，以公开证明他们有有效的数据要出售。
 这些是英国业务管理咨询机构的主管人员和美国服装和配饰制造商总裁的登录详细信息。
卖方拒绝分享他如何获得登录凭证，但表示他还有数百笔待售。
 根据威胁情报公司KELA提供的数据，同一威胁参与者先前曾表示有意购买“ Azor日志”，该术语是指从感染了AzorUltinfo-stealer特洛伊木马的计算机收集的数据。
 Infostealer日志几乎总是包含木马从受感染主机上安装的浏览器中提取的用户名和密码。
 这些数据通常由信息窃取者运营商收集，他们会对其进行过滤和组织，然后将其出售给Genesis等专门市场，黑客论坛，或者将其出售给其他网络犯罪团伙。
 KELA产品经理Raveed Laeb对ZDNet表示：“受损的公司电子邮件凭据对于网络犯罪分子而言很有价值，因为它们可以通过许多不同的方式获利。
 “攻击者可以将其用于内部通信，这是'CEO骗局'的一部分-犯罪分子操纵员工为他们提供大笔金钱；可以将其用于勒索方案中以获取敏感信息；或者，这些凭据还可以利用此漏洞来访问需要基于电子邮件的2FA的其他内部系统，以便在组织中横向移动并进行网络入侵。”
 但是，最有可能的是，被感染的电子邮件将被收购并滥用为CEO骗局，也称为BEC骗局。根据联邦调查局（FBI）今年的一份报告，到目前为止，BEC诈骗是2019年最受欢迎的网络犯罪形式，占去年报告的网络犯罪损失的一半。
防止黑客从任何类型的被盗凭据中获利的最简单方法是对您的在线帐户使用两步验证（2SV）或两因素身份验证（2FA）解决方案。即使黑客设法窃取了登录详细信息，但如果没有适当的2SV / 2FA附加验证程序，它们也将无用。
note_en:
A threat actor is currently selling passwords for the email accounts of hundreds of C-level executives at companies across the world.
     The data is being sold on a closed-access underground forum for Russian-speaking hackers named  Exploit.in,  ZDNet has learned this week.
 The threat actor is selling email and password combinations for Office 365 and Microsoft accounts, which he claims are owned by high-level executives occupying functions such as:
  Access to any of these accounts is sold for prices ranging from $100 to $1,500, depending on the company size and user's role.
  A source in the cyber-security community who agreed to contact the seller to obtain samples has confirmed the validity of the data and obtained valid credentials for two accounts, the CEO of a US medium-sized software company and the CFO of an EU-based retail store chain.
 The source, which requested that ZDNet not use its name, is in the process of notifying the two companies, but also two other companies for which the seller published account passwords as public proof that they had valid data to sell.
 These were login details for an executive at a UK business management consulting agency and for the president of a US apparel and accessories maker.
    The seller refused to share how he obtained the login credentials but said he had hundreds more to sell.
 According to data provided by threat intelligence firm  KELA, the same threat actor had previously expressed interest in buying "Azor logs," a term that refers to data collected from computers infected with the  AzorUlt info-stealer trojan.
 Infostealer logs almost always contain usernames and passwords that the trojan extracts from browsers found installed on infected hosts.
 This data is often collected by the infostealer operators, who filter and organize it, and then put it on sale on dedicated markets like Genesis, on hacking forums, or they sell it to other cybercrime gangs.
 "Compromised corporate email credentials can be valuable for cybercriminals, as they can be monetized in many different ways," KELA Product Manager Raveed Laeb told  ZDNet.
 "Attackers can use them for internal communications as part of a 'CEO scam' - where criminals manipulate employees into wiring them large sums of money; they can be used in order to access sensitive information as part of an extortion scheme; or, these credentials can also be exploited in order to gain access to other internal systems that require email-based 2FA, in order to move laterally in the organization and conduct a network intrusion," Laeb added.
 But, most likely, the compromised emails will be bought and abused for CEO scams, also known as BEC scams. According to an FBI report this year, BEC scams were, by far, the most popular form of cybercrime in 2019, having accounted for  half of the cybercrime losses reported last year.
 The easiest way of preventing hackers from monetizing any type of stolen credentials is to use a two-step verification (2SV) or two-factor authentication (2FA) solution for your online accounts. Even if hackers manage to steal login details, they will be useless without the proper 2SV/2FA additional verifier.

note:
这是气候科学家们对一项令人震惊的气候研究的回应，这是令人震惊的信息，许多专家称，上周该研究在社交媒体上广为流传。
 是的，仍然有希望防止灾难性的全球变暖。他们说，是的，将温室气体排放量减少到零仍然可以阻止气候变化。
 该论文发表在《科学报告》杂志上，表明世界已经跨越了不可逆转的气候门槛。结论是，即使明天停止所有温室气体排放，世界仍将继续变暖数百年。的确，作者说，为了防止失控的变暖，在1960年代必须停止排放。
 在这种可怕的情况下，停止变暖的唯一希望就是从大气中吸收大量的二氧化碳。
  该论文表明，一系列复杂的反馈循环导致了无法阻止的变暖。北极冰融化，使地球吸收更多的热量。温度升高导致大气中存有更多的水。多年冻土迅速融化，在此过程中将二氧化碳和甲烷倒入空气中。这些额外的碳排放会加剧变暖。
 至少可以这样说，这是一个惊人的场景。上周它催生了许多可怕的头条新闻，警告地球已经过去了。
  在过去一周中，许多气候科学家公开批评了该论文。他们的大多数反对意见都以研究所基于的模型为中心。
本文依赖于来自单个地球系统模型的仿真，并且在此基础上进行了简单的仿真。该模型不属于气候科学家在这类研究中通常使用的一套气候和地球系统模型。相反，该模型是由研究的两位作者，挪威的商学院教授JørgenRanders和UlrichGolüke设计的。
 由于该模型非常简单，因此在很大程度上依赖于有关地球及其气候系统的内置假设。而且其中一些关键假设似乎与更先进的地球系统模型通常对行星的建议相抵触。
 加州奥克兰突破性气候研究所能源与气候主管Zeke Hausfather说，例如，随着地球变暖，水蒸气在空气中的保持方式与其他模型趋向于“矛盾”。
 由于甲烷排放量的增加，它预测的变暖量也可能太强了。
 豪斯法特说：“对于永久冻土的假设以及由此产生的排放量也有一些批评。”
 例如，模拟假设永久冻土迅速融化，并且释放的所有碳都以甲烷的形式出来，而甲烷是一种比二氧化碳强大得多的温室气体。
 科罗拉多大学博尔德分校的多年冻土专家Merritt Turetsky将E＆E News指向她为回应这项研究而发布的Twitter帖子。
扣上永冻土！本文是一项思想实验，依赖于我对多年冻土C释放的经验和建模工作。首先，我♥️使用模型进行思想实验，因此在这里是肯定的。以下是永久冻土THAW的样子-太复杂了！ 1 / pic.twitter.com/zlFYMrHAXY
—梅里特·特雷茨基博士（@queenofpeat）2020年11月12日
 她指出，多年冻土有可能在未来逐渐融化。其排放将包括二氧化碳和甲烷，主要是二氧化碳。 Turetsky在推文中说，本文假设的甲烷量“很傻”。
 她补充说，永久冻土的一些排放物可能会被北极植物重新吸收一些碳而抵消。
 这并不是说永久冻土不值得密切关注。许多科学家一致认为，永久冻土解冻是未来气候变化预测中的主要不确定因素-科学家们仍在努力了解随着地球变暖，永久冻土融化的速度有多快，以及它将释放多少碳。
 也就是说，多年冻土在许多模型中都“非常粗糙”地表示，Turetsky指出。因此，在气候模拟中肯定有继续改进其位置的空间。
 但是专家们普遍认为，上周研究中使用的简单模型产生了不切实际的假设。
埃克塞特大学的气候科学家理查德·贝茨（Richard Betts）指出，该论文所依据的模型具有“重大缺陷”，并建议其关于水蒸气以及融化冰雪对气候的影响的假设可能太强了。
 该特定模型中的水蒸气和地表反照率反馈要比IPCC中使用的任何CMIP模型都要强得多，后者通过观察和物理理解可以更紧密地获知。此外，论文的两个图之间也存在奇怪的矛盾之处
-理查德·贝茨（Richard Betts）（@richardabetts）2020年11月12日
 苏黎世联邦理工学院（ETH Zurich）的气候科学家Reto Knutti确认该模型关于多年冻土的假设与更复杂的模型不一致，并指出其他研究发现将温室气体减少到零可以阻止全球变暖。
 加州大学洛杉矶分校的气候科学家丹尼尔·斯温（Daniel Swain）也指出，该研究的标题包含一个错误-当公认的科学术语为“融化”时，它是指“融化”永久冻土。
 强烈反对之后，《科学报告》修改了先前发布的有关该研究的新闻稿。它说：“作者鼓励其他研究人员使用替代模型探索他们的结果。”
 自上周世界末日头条新闻出现以来，科学家们的强烈反应也得到了许多媒体的报道。
Hausfather指出，扩大一个不切实际的严峻气候前景会带来真正的风险。
 “有一个非常有害的想法，就是时不时地抬起头来，这已经来不及了-我们已经无可挽回了；无论我们做什么，我们都陷入了某种失控的气候变化，“ 他说。 “这通常不会被科学以任何方式，形状或形式支持。”
 Hausfather警告说，这种信息可能暗示气候行动是徒劳的，无法采取任何措施来避免即将来临的灾难。错了
 气候变化仍然是对地球的生存威胁，持续的变暖只会加剧已经在地球上蔓延的后果-海平面上升，冰融化，热浪加剧和更多极端天气事件，仅举几例。但是研究始终表明，抑制温室气体排放可能会阻止全球变暖。
 奥斯法德说：“像这样的事情，不能代表在这些问题上的共识，并向厄运主义者的人群提供弹药，这是有问题的。”
note_en:
That's the overwhelming message from climate scientists in response to an alarming climate study that many experts described as flawed when it splashed across social media last week.
 Yes, there's still hope to prevent catastrophic global warming. And yes, reducing greenhouse gas emissions to zero could still halt climate change, they said.
 Published in the journal  Scientific Reports, the paper suggests the world has crossed an irreversible climate threshold. Even if all greenhouse gas emissions were stopped tomorrow, it concludes, the world would continue warming for hundreds of years. Indeed, the authors said that emissions would have had to stop in the 1960s to prevent runaway warming.
 In this dire scenario, the only hope of stopping the warming would be to suck large volumes of carbon dioxide out of the atmosphere.
  The paper suggests that a complicated series of feedback cycles led to unstoppable warming. Arctic ice melts away, allowing the Earth to absorb more heat. Rising temperatures cause the atmosphere to hold more water. Permafrost rapidly thaws, pouring carbon dioxide and methane into the air in the process. These extra carbon emissions drive even more warming.
 It's a striking scenario, to say the least. And it spawned a number of dire headlines last week, warning that the Earth had passed a point of no return.
  Over the past week, numerous climate scientists have publicly criticized the paper. Most of their objections center on the model the study is based on.
 The paper relies on simulations from a single Earth system model — and a simple one at that. The model is not among the suite of climate and Earth system models typically used by climate scientists in these kinds of studies. Rather, the model was designed by the study's authors, Jørgen Randers and Ulrich Golüke, two business school professors in Norway.
 Because the model is so simple, it relies heavily on built-in assumptions about the Earth and its climate system. And some of those key assumptions seem to conflict with what more advanced Earth system models typically suggest about the planet.
 The way water vapor is held in the air as the Earth warms, for instance, is "at odds" with what other models tend to simulate, according to Zeke Hausfather, director of climate and energy at the Breakthrough Institute in Oakland, Calif.
 The amount of warming it projects in response to rising methane emissions is probably too strong, as well.
 "There's also some criticism of the permafrost assumptions they make and how much emissions come from that," Hausfather said.
 For instance, the simulations assume that permafrost thaws rapidly and that all the carbon it releases comes out in the form of methane, a significantly more potent greenhouse gas than carbon dioxide.
 Merritt Turetsky, a permafrost expert at the University of Colorado, Boulder, pointed E&E News to a  Twitter thread she posted in response to the study.
 Buckle up for a permafrost ride! This paper is a thought experiment that relies on my empirical & modeling work on permafrost C release. First, I ♥️ using models for thought experiments so that is a positive here. Below is what permafrost THAW looks like - it's complicated! 1/  pic.twitter.com/zlFYMrHAXY
— Dr. Merritt Turetsky (@queenofpeat)  November 12, 2020
 Permafrost is likely to thaw gradually in the future, she noted. And its emissions will include both carbon dioxide and methane, mostly CO2. The amount of methane assumed in this paper is "silly," Turetsky tweeted.
 Furthermore, she added, some of the emissions from permafrost will likely be offset by Arctic plants that reabsorb some of that carbon.
 None of this is to say that permafrost isn't worth keeping a close eye on. Many scientists agree that thawing permafrost is a major uncertainty in projections about future climate change — scientists are still working to understand how quickly permafrost is likely to thaw as the planet warms and how much carbon it will emit.
 That said, permafrost is represented "very crudely" in a lot of models, Turetsky noted. So there's definitely room to keep improving its place in climate simulations.
 But experts generally agree that the simple model used in the study last week produced unrealistic assumptions.
  Richard Betts, a climate scientist at the University of Exeter, noted that the paper relies on a model with "major flaws" and  suggested that its assumptions about water vapor and the effects of melting snow and ice on the climate are likely too strong.
 The water vapour and surface albedo feedbacks in that particular model are much stronger than in any of the CMIP models used in IPCC, which are more closely informed by observations & physical understanding  Also there's strange inconsistencies between two of the paper's figures
— Richard Betts (@richardabetts)  November 12, 2020
 Reto Knutti, a climate scientist at ETH Zurich,  affirmed that the model's assumptions about permafrost are inconsistent with more complex models and noted that other studies find that reducing greenhouse gases to zero stops global warming.
 Daniel Swain, a climate scientist at UCLA, also  pointed out that the study's title contains an error — it refers to "melting" permafrost, when the accepted scientific term is "thawing."
 Following the backlash,  Scientific Reports amended a  press release it had previously published about the study. "The authors encourage other researchers to explore their results using alternative models," it said.
 Since the initial wave of doomsday headlines last week, the strong response from scientists has also been reported by various outlets.
 There are real risks that come with amplifying an unrealistically grim climate future, Hausfather noted.
 "There's a really pernicious idea that sort of rears its head every now and then, that it's sort of too late — we're past the point of no return; we're locked into some sort of runaway climate change no matter what we do," he said. "That's generally not supported by the science in any way, shape or form."
 This kind of messaging can imply that climate action is futile and that nothing can be done to avert the coming disaster, Hausfather warned. That's false.
 Climate change remains an existential threat to the planet, and continued warming will only worsen the consequences already playing out across the planet — rising sea levels, thawing ice, intensifying heat waves and more extreme weather events, to name a few. But research consistently indicates that curbing greenhouse gas emissions could stop global warming.
 "Anything like this, that is not representative of the consensus on these matters and gives ammunition to the doomist crowd, is problematic," Hausfather said.
title: 世界末日气候研究被研究人员揭穿
title_en: Doomsday climate study debunked by researchers

在夏威夷大岛西海岸海底下发现大量的淡水，可能解除居住在那里的人们遭受干旱的威胁。
 夏威夷大学的埃里克·阿提亚斯（Eric Attias）和他的同事们使用类似于MRI扫描的成像技术，发现了该水库，该水库包含在到达海床以下至少500米的多孔岩石中。
 他们用一条船拖着一条40米长的天线在其后方产生电磁场，从而通过海和海底以下发送电流。由于海水比淡水更好，因此研究小组可以区分两者。他们发现，该水库距海岸至少4公里，包含3.5立方公里的淡水。
   夏威夷的大部分淡水来自陆上含水层，这些含水层是地下的岩石和土壤层，降雨后会收集水。研究小组认为，从这些含水层流出的水补充了这个新发现的水库。
  气候变化导致许多地方的干旱加剧，这可能使某些地区缺水。在夏威夷，降雨减少和森林遭到破坏可能意味着陆上含水层最终干.。
 离岸水库不仅可以缓解干旱，而且比陆上含水层更容易抽水，因为水处于高压状态。 Attias说，访问它也将对周围的生态系统产生最小的影响。
 研究小组说，类似的水库可能位于其他火山岛以外，这可以缓解因气候变化而面临缺水威胁的其他地方。
纽约哥伦比亚大学的克里·凯说，通常是通过钻探来抽取样品来发现新的淡水来源，但是Attias和他的同事使用的新成像技术可以使这一过程变得更加容易和便宜。
note_en:
A huge cache of fresh water found beneath the sea floor off the western coast of Hawaii’s Big Island could lift the threat of drought for people living there.
 Eric Attias at the University of Hawaii and his colleagues discovered the reservoir, which is contained in porous rock reaching at least 500 metres beneath the sea floor, using an imaging technique similar to an  MRI scan.
 They used a boat towing a 40-metre-long antenna behind it to generate an electromagnetic field, sending an electric current through the sea and below the sea floor. As seawater is a better conductor than fresh water, the team could distinguish between the two. They found that the reservoir extends at least 4 kilometres from the coast and contains 3.5 cubic kilometres of fresh water.
   Most of Hawaii’s fresh water comes from onshore aquifers, which are layers of rock and soil underground that collect water after rainfall. The team believes that this newfound reservoir is replenished by water flowing out of these aquifers.
  Climate change has lead to increasing droughts in many places,  which could leave some areas without water. In Hawaii, decreased rainfall and the destruction of forests could mean the onshore aquifers eventually dry up.
 Not only would the offshore reservoir help relieve drought, it may also be easier to pump from than the onshore aquifers, because the water is under high pressure. Accessing it would also have minimal impact on surrounding ecosystems, says Attias.
 Similar caches of water may be located off other volcanic islands, says the team, which could provide a relief for other places threatened by water scarcity due to climate change.
 New sources of fresh water are normally discovered by drilling to extract samples, but the new imaging technique used by Attias and his colleagues could make this process easier and cheaper, says Kerry Key at Columbia University in New York.
title: 在夏威夷海底发现巨大的淡水水库
title_en: Huge Reservoir of Fresh Water Found Beneath the Sea Off Hawaii

note:
方舟YELLVILLE —是10月在奥扎克。草已经干dried了，树木变成了古铜色和褐色。鹿躺在迷彩的皮卡车后面，眼神呆滞。每个星期五晚上，高中橄榄球头盔都会破裂。一周七天，加工厂的工人正在为全国的感恩节餐桌杀害，毁坏，拔毛和捆火鸡。
 这个寒冷多雨的周末，在耶维尔（Yellville），到处都是火鸡-火鸡衬衫和火鸡服装以及火鸡用具。有一个抽奖活动将鸟送给感恩节晚餐。火鸡腿也很活跃，被抽出了烟熏桶。在演奏台上，一名法官宣布“鼓槌小姐”比赛的获胜者，她在选美比赛中闪闪发光。 “这是鼓槌小姐，因为他们正在判断谁的大腿最好，”一个年纪较大的女人向我解释说。
 但是-这是不寻常的，而且令许多当地人感到沮丧和震惊，这没有活火鸡。没有一个人关在笼子里被拖在皮卡后面。没有人从法院屋顶扔掉。没有人放弃演奏台，被尖叫的少年接住了。没有人从飞机上掉下来。这就是Yellville Turkey Trot节最著名和最臭名昭著的地方：生活，呼吸，鸣叫的鸟儿从低空飞行的飞机上跳下来。
  似乎值得一提的土耳其不会飞。尽管您在郊区道路上的羊群中看到的野生，深色羽毛的动物能够飞进树木之间以及树木之间，但是您在感恩节食用的工厂化养殖的白羽毛动物却更靠近禽类的企鹅一侧。飞行能力谱。如果它们发现自己可以从500英尺高处自由落体，它们可以通过狂扑扑腾来减慢下降速度，并随风飘扬。但是，有些人死于带有无用翅膀的肉砧。
 在过去的七十年中，每年一次，只有几次休息，耶维尔（Yellville）就有十二只左右的禽鸟展示了这种引力的现实。组织者和与会人员解释说，土耳其小跑是一个备受期待的活动，人们拥有很多奥扎克人的骄傲，但没有很多钱，组织者和与会者都解释了这是一次过分的活动，当地人喜欢爱，爱恨，爱去，真爱谈谈关于。 “在费耶特维尔，有一个大型节日。他们在那儿有个摊位，您可以在那儿走，然后停下来走，‘好吃的牛，整洁的’。”当地一家撤退物业的所有者鲍勃·金（Bob King）告诉我。 “我们是一个小镇的节日。这对人们很重要。”
 然而，这种传统可能像在街上吸烟和卖火鸡的火鸡一样死气沉沉。多年以来，媒体的负面和嘲弄，动物福利组织及其支持者的批评以及各种监管和法律机构的参与，导致周末的活火鸡部分关闭，也许是永久关闭。
  随着口头相声竞赛的回响和the缝的行会炫耀其商品，一些人担心周末的其他一切也会枯萎。地方企业主担心，重要的收入来源已经消失了，有些人希望能出现一架架着飞鸟的飞机，警察，政客，专栏作家和素食主义者都该死。商会的凯斯·埃德蒙兹说：“我们将不得不看到数字的结局。”每次飞机从头顶飞过时，小孩都会检查鸟是否会飞出来。
结束一个小镇钟爱的年度活动，从一小会儿混乱的恐怖中拯救几只鸟，是否值得？考虑到成百上千的肉食鸟类面临着更加可怕的生与死，这是否有意义？考虑到奥扎克人这个角落居民的顽强意志以及美国人对吃肉和寒冷天气的奉献精神，这种做法会坚持吗？
 我不确定去参加音乐节之前，甚至不确定。但是我知道这一点：这不是关于扔掉一只不会飞出飞机的鸟的感恩节故事。这是一个感恩节的故事，讲述了人类抛弃不会飞出飞机的鸟的意志。
  在该国仍然重要的传统地区，人民也是大保守主义者和小保守主义者的地方，T是传统。当第二次世界大战结束时，该镇就开始了，当时该镇的美国退伍军人协会赞助了秋季火鸡通话比赛，并赠送了火鸡赠品。几年后，一名当地飞行员从飞机上扔了几只火鸡给下面聚集的人群，给了比赛翅膀。
 土耳其小跑的起源很愚蠢。他们太离谱了。但是他们也很高贵，所以故事就这样了。一位历史人士说：“当天的活动原本是一场野火鸡保护活动，引起人们对阿肯色州日益减少的火鸡种群的关注。”几只鸟本应在厚厚的欧扎克（Ozark）树林中生存和繁衍，而另一些则回家准备晚餐。
 在接下来的几十年中，土耳其小跑仍然是家乡，口耳相传，几乎每个Yellville居民和周边地区的许多人每年都会涌向市中心一次，以供他们吃饭，聊天，也许与他们离开一只鸟。与一些老年人告诉我的相反，这次活动似乎并不是辛辛那提旧情景喜剧《 WKRP》那一集的灵感来源。扔掉一只不会飞出飞机的鸟是一种奇特的想法，这种想法显然已经打动了一个美国人。
  当国家询问者注意到时，情况开始发生变化。在1980年代后期，这家小报派了两名记者到火鸡下落，并进行了题为“这是病！ Yellville Turkeys从飞机上摔下来，是为了娱乐。”这个故事描述了鸟类坠入死亡的“噩梦般的场面”。
 一只火鸡猛地撞入了电源线，电线弯曲了大约三英尺，然后才折断。鸟撞到地面，震惊并晕眩，然后试图走路……可怜地试图用两条明显折断的腿奔跑，然后被一堆小孩压死。一群小鸟追捕了其中的那只鸟，并为之争斗-在一次激烈的拔河比赛中使用了这只鸟，直到一个男孩将火鸡的翅膀撕掉后才结束。
（这个奇怪的新闻的一个奇怪的附录：我依靠的是《问询者》文章的二手版本。该出版物的档案馆被炭疽病污染后被摧毁。）
 散文是紫色的，描写过度。但不是很多。此后几年中记录的大量文献证据以及许多城镇居民的证词表明，过去和现在，当您将火鸡从空中数百英尺高处掉下时，会发生这种情况。惊慌失措的动物试图纠正自己。有些阵阵风。其他人没有。有些人落地时死亡。其他人幸存的骨头骨折。当其他人被当地孩子争夺时，他们遭受了严重的伤害。一些明显的震惊消失了。值得一提的是，有些杂乱无章，但没有受到伤害。
  罗斯·希利亚德（Rose Hilliard）说：“我当时站在镇上一幢建筑物后面的小巷里，那架飞机正好飞过头顶。” “我们都在努力追逐它，我在想，天哪，我不能超过15个孩子！不可能。他们不应该将它们扔到城镇上去。他们应该在那条河对面的小河对岸。但是飞行员有点以为他们不会做错事，对此感到自豪。”她告诉我，她遇到的火鸡承受着热的压力，感到震惊和瘀青。 “看着受惊的动物试图逃离人群并不有趣。那是-我不称它为娱乐。”
 但这是以一种奇怪而引人注目的方式。问询者发表文章之后，火鸡的下落不仅成为当地媒体的主食，而且成为了全国媒体的迷恋。随之而来的报道令人震惊，嘲笑，屈服和屈膝-《土耳其小跑》被形容为加油站杂货店的那种秋天的节日，是人类对动物痛苦的喜悦的巴洛克例子，你可以相信它吗？
  不仅是媒体注意到了。负责残酷调查部门的达芙娜·纳奇米诺维奇（Daphna Nachminovitch）告诉我，动物道德对待人士早在20多年前就知道了这一事件，此后一直对此表示不满。她对我说：“当比尔·克林顿（Bill Clinton）担任阿肯色州州长时，我们开始收到有关它的投诉。” “当地人的投诉。我们一直都知道这一点。听起来好像有人在编造，因为谁会认为将活体动物从飞机上丢掉很有趣？”
  向该事件的关注者传达了该事件的信息。它向活动的组织者请愿。它还提供了5,000美元的信息，以逮捕坠落飞行员的飞行员，并在某一时刻雇用了一名私人调查员调查事情。莫特利克鲁（MötleyCrüe）的汤米·李（Tommy Lee）等人努力阻止下降。他在一封公开信中写道：“阿肯色州提供了许多很酷的户外聚会，这些聚会不依赖虐待狂。” “我正在写信，向成千上万的其他人表达自己的声音，请您帮助深化这病态的特技表演。”
 媒体的关注促进了激进主义者的关注，政治关注以及各种地方和国家机构的召唤，以查明是否存在防止飞鸟和飞机零件的法律手段。但是，动物残暴法令人吃惊地松懈，尤其是在农场动物方面。它们是特定于物种和目的的，这意味着科学家也许可以合法地对研究中的老鼠进行观察，但不能对她的宠物狗进行观察。而且，当然，它们需要被强制执行，而通常情况下却不是。如果您想要便宜的汉堡，则需要一定程度的残酷对待动物，大多数美国人在面对这种动物时会认为这很恐怖。
希利亚德在当地治安官办公室投诉。她告诉我：“我实际上必须这样做。” “我对飞机的飞行员提出了虐待动物的投诉。他们对此一无所获。他们说，‘好吧，在做任何事情之前，我们都必须抓住他。”最终，她说，“他们几乎把我引爆了。”
  动物权利激进主义者还考虑让联邦航空管理局介入，因为让美国人飞来飞去，将农畜从人口中心附近的飞机上赶下来是不明智的。去年，美国联邦航空局发言人林恩·伦斯福德（Lynn Lunsford）表示，该机构无权提出要求。她说：“ ​​FAA法规并未专门针对将活体动物从飞机上掉下来的行为进行处理，因此我们无权禁止这种行为。”内华达州民主党代表迪娜·提图斯（Dina Titus）试图促使美国联邦航空局采取行动，但那一事无成。
  当我就各种法律机构试图阻止的和未能阻止的人们采访人们时，我惊叹于实际上对于需要预防的内容所知甚少。这些是谁的火鸡？谁安排了活动？谁知道
 当我问这些问题时，小镇的omertà开始了。一位接待员告诉我，耶维尔市市长不愿对此事发表评论，因为该镇与之无关。她说：“这是由私人团体举办的私人活动。”不必担心公立学校为庆祝这一天而关闭，或者鸟儿被扔到法院的屋顶上，或者镇上的警察似乎会这么做。有管辖权。埃德蒙兹说，商会与火鸡掉下来无关。近年来，从事高空工作的人是一名自称“幻影飞行员”的人。
  然而，出现了一种有条件的从句，被动语态，对我的理解。这些鸟可能来自后院式的操作，而不是大型农场或拍卖行。他们可能是驯化的传统鸟类，而不是一些耶维尔居民认为的野生鸟类。有几个人可能已经帮助他们将它们装载到另一个人的卡车后面的敞开式拖车中。然后可能去了当地的简易机场，因为也许有人坐飞机，以为整个事情都是a的。想象一下，那个锡罐里有多响！
 政治和媒体对ho窃的关注变得过多。商会成员开始收到有关该事件的成千上万条消息，有些甚至是威胁。 “本地企业正在关闭其服务器，” Edmonds告诉我。 “他们受到了可靠的威胁：'我希望您的孩子死于恐怖，痛苦的死亡，就好像他们被从飞机上扔掉一样。'”今年春天，商会决定不再赞助土耳其小跑，这是一致决定的。但是流泪了。扶轮社说，如果该场所的活火鸡为零，它将接任赞助商。
 将太多的注意力放在一个小镇的小规模（如果不好的话）的传统上，是否为时过早？纳奇米诺维奇（Nachminovitch）说，重点永远不会阻止该镇举行秋季节日或以其认为合适的方式进行庆祝。她说：“有一种想法，就是我们用鼻子轻视他们，但事实并非如此。” “这是关于火鸡的。关于虐待动物。这是关于人类的礼节。在虐待动物的地方，这就是我们的地方。”
但是在城里，被铸成土耳其小跑传奇的反派。 “恭喜，您的追随者在暴力威胁和极端主义的压抑下ca不休，在成功72年后杀死了土耳其小跑节。”当地报纸《百特公报》的摄影记者乔什·杜利（Josh Dooley）写道。 “就像您为灭绝各种动物而谴责的人和做法一样，您杀死了某些东西却不了解它，甚至试图理解您造成的破坏。”
 不过，音乐节的双方总是有很强的游击队员。许多人认为这给小镇留下了不好的印象。一些人感到恶心。 “让我们面对现实吧：这只是简单的意思。我们是什么样的人？”阅读了2018年4月在阿肯色州民主党公报上给编辑的一封信。 “只要他们能说，火鸡就不会吞噬请求以免被迫逃跑，因此他们可能会人道地加入1亿只火鸡，享受上帝赋予的命运……使他们瘦弱的脖子从不适合飞行的路上割断下来。 ，大大增加了排长胸的身体”。
    T说得很对。种禽的情况更糟。在阿肯色州的这个角落，人们对这种现实很了解，该地区产生了大量的鸟类，最终出现在感恩节餐桌上。附近的奥扎克（Ozark）镇被称为“以火鸡为食的镇”。 Butterball目前正在Yellville郊外建造一家大型工厂。
  产蛋鸡可能会在农场动物惨痛的奥运会中夺金。雄性小鸡被摔死。雌性小鸡长大后被视为母鸡放在盒子里，它们是如此之小，以至于无法伸展翅膀，无法进行任何使鸡感觉像鸡的活动。尽管鸡的寿命可以超过十年，但如果脱垂，败血病或食人症不会首先杀死鸡，那么一旦产蛋速度降低一两年，便会杀死蛋鸡。
 土耳其只好一点了。家禽出生在孵化场，他们会去仓库度过余生，随着它们变得越来越大，情况变得越来越狭窄。由于被压紧的压力会导致它们互相啄，有时甚至会导致死亡，因此鸟类的喙和脚趾部分被剪断。汤姆们也消除了他们的鼻子-垂在喙附近的五颜六色的肉。然后它们长大，站在自己的尿液和粪便中，吃着药丸。许多人发生疮和骨折。很少接受个性化的医疗服务。身受重伤的火鸡死于饥饿的情况并非闻所未闻。
 在去往屠​​宰场的路上，它们被塞进笼子里，这个过程经常导致骨头断裂。 （由于压碎，暴露或其他原因，每年有100万只火鸡死于屠宰场。）到达目的地时，它们的脚被倒挂着，骨头断裂了，然后才将其头部浸泡在带电的水中。他们的喉咙被割伤了。像鸡一样，土耳其也不属于《人道屠杀方法法》的涵盖范围。这意味着对严重虐待火鸡的公司几乎没有处罚。生产线的运行速度如此之快，以至于有时刀片将鸟切成活禽，而将它们浸泡在沸腾的水中则还活着。
  他们的身体不比人类对这些身体的残酷程度高。您可能还记得上小学的时候，土耳其人是美洲人。驯化发生在数百年前，甚至数千年前。就在耶维尔（Yellville Turkey）小跑开始时，在1940年代，大型农业公司开始密集地饲养驯养的火鸡，以便更快地从它们那里获取更多的肉。实验取得了惊人的成功：宽胸白鸟，很可能是感恩节餐桌上的那只鸟，从1960年的15磅重的鸟变成了今天的30磅重的鸟，并且体重增长得更快比自然预期
这些是弗兰肯生物，例如斗牛犬或赛马。它们不能自然繁殖，并且身体与健康长寿不相容。这些鸟即使没有被宰杀，也会面临骨骼，心脏和呼吸系统的怪异问题，因为它们卡通般超大的胸部会张开并向身体施加压力。一家大型的农场动物救助组织Farm Sanctuary的国家庇护所主任Susie Coston说：“它们会因这些复合物骨折和腿部受伤而常常无法修复，因为它们生长得如此之快。” “它们太大了。”
 关于它们的另一件奇怪的事情：鸟类患有强迫性满足感。如果您把食物留给他们随意吃，“他们永远都不会停止吃，”科斯顿告诉我。 “我从未见过类似的东西。他们吃得太多，以致伤害了肠道。”
  她说，如果您扣留食物，而这些食物是维持他们生存所必需的，那么他们总是很饿。
   在耶维尔（Yellville），确实有一些鸟类逃脱，按照活动的创立者的意图，尽管它们只是由人类铸造而成，但只是短暂地使奥扎克人繁衍。有些被养成宠物。有些进入农场庇护所。距奥扎克斯（Ozarks）几百英里处的地方是一个小型救援农场，由莉萨·麦克唐纳（Lisa McDonald）和乔·迪南（Joe Dinan）管理，后者还经营着一家啤酒厂，并表现出一种脆弱的友好精神。
 Sweet Bear Rescue Farm的非人类居民包括一头猪鬃猪，偶尔会出现腹部发痒的情况。两只山羊；各种各样的鸡，分成两个交战团伙；和一些毛茸茸的狗。我参观了农场，遇见了在耶维尔（Yellville）幸存下来的火鸡。志愿者将他抱起，并帮助将他和他的一些弟兄带到农场保护区，该保护区派出了一对夫妇居住在Sweet Bear。火鸡以甲壳虫乐队的名字命名为保罗和乔治。
 当我和麦当劳一起走进谷仓前的围场时，跟着迪斯尼公主般的鸡，狗和猪，乔治走了出来与我们见面。麦当劳说：“他爱人们，”乔治在像孔雀一样陈列时，他扑出虹彩的羽毛时脸上的肉肉部分变色了。麦当劳掏出一个特百惠（Tupperware）容器，里面装满西红柿片，用手给他喂食，他像疯子一样啄食。她告诉我：“他爱蓝莓和黑莓-这些是他最喜欢的，最喜欢的东西。”
  现在他变成了火鸡，做着一些农场动物很少得到的火鸡
note_en:
YELLVILLE, Ark. —It is October in the Ozarks. The grass has dried out and the trees have bronzed and browned. Deer lie glaze-eyed in the back of camouflaged pickup trucks. High-school football helmets crack every Friday night. And seven days a week, workers in processing plants are helping to kill, gut, pluck, and truss turkeys for Thanksgiving tables around the country.
 Here in Yellville this cold and rainy weekend, there are turkeys everywhere—turkey shirts and turkey costumes and turkey paraphernalia. There is a raffle giving away birds for Thanksgiving dinner. There’s a brisk trade in turkey legs, too, pulled out of a barrel smoker. At the bandstand, a judge announces the winner of the “Miss Drumsticks” contest, who gleams and sparkles in her pageant finery. “It’s Miss Drumsticks because they’re judging who has the best  thighs,” an older woman explained to me, matter-of-fact.
 But—and this is unusual, and much to the dismay and consternation of many locals—there are no live turkeys. None in a cage towed behind a pickup. None thrown from the courthouse roof. None pitched off the bandstand and picked up by screaming teenagers. And none dropped out of an airplane. That is what the Yellville Turkey Trot festival is famous and infamous for, you see: living, breathing, squawking birds getting lobbed out of a low-flying aircraft.
  Turkeys, it seems worth mentioning, do not fly. Although the wild, dark-feathered ones you see in flocks on exurban roads are capable of fluttering up into and in between trees, the factory-farm-bred, white-feathered ones you eat on Thanksgiving are closer to the penguin side of the avian flying-ability spectrum. The birds can slow their descent by flapping wildly and catch the wind and glide, should they find themselves free-falling from 500 feet. But some die on impact, fleshy anvils with useless wings.
 Once a year for the past seven decades, with just a few breaks, Yellville has had a dozen or so fowl demonstrate this gravitational reality. The Turkey Trot is a much-anticipated event for people with a lot of Ozark pride but without a lot of money, organizers and attendees explained—a transgressive event that locals love to love, love to hate, love to go to, and really love to talk about. “There’s a festival that goes on in Fayetteville that’s huge. They have booths there where you walk up and you just stop in your tracks and go, ‘Holy cow, that’s neat!’” Bob King, the owner of a local  retreat property, told me. “We’re a  small-town festival. It is important to people.”
 Yet the tradition might be as dead as the turkeys whose legs were getting smoked and sold in the street. Years of negative and mocking media attention, criticism from animal-welfare groups and their supporters, and the involvement of a variety of regulatory and legal authorities have led to the live-turkey part of the weekend getting shut down, perhaps for good.
  As the lip-synch contest echoed and the quilting guild showed off its wares, some worried that the everything-else portion of the weekend would wither away, too. Local-business owners fretted that a vital source of income was gone—with some hoping that a plane foisting the birds would show up, cops and politicians and op-ed writers and vegan busybodies be damned. “We will have to see how the numbers end up,” said Keith Edmonds of the chamber of commerce, a note of resignation in his voice. Every time an aircraft passed overhead, little kids checked to see if a bird would come out.
  Was it worth it, ending a town’s beloved annual event to save a few birds from a few moments of confused terror? Was it meaningful, given how many billions of birds raised for meat face a far more gruesome life and death? Would it stick, given the steeliness of the residents of this corner of the Ozarks and the devotion of Americans to their meat-eating and cold-weather traditions?
 I was not sure before going to the festival, and I was even less sure after it. But I knew this: This was not a Thanksgiving story about throwing a bird that does not fly out of an airplane. This was a Thanksgiving story about the human will to throw a bird that does not fly out of an airplane.
  T  is tradition in a part of the country where tradition still matters, where the people are big- C Conservative and small- c conservative, too: That was a consistent refrain I heard in Yellville. It got started as World War II ended, when the town’s American Legion post sponsored an autumn turkey-calling contest with a turkey giveaway. A few years later, a local pilot tossed some turkeys from a plane for the crowd assembled below, giving the event its wings.
 The origins of the Turkey Trot were silly. They were outrageous. But they were also noble, or so the story goes. “The day’s activities were intended to be a wild turkey–conservation activity, calling attention to Arkansas’s dwindling turkey population,” according to  one history. A few birds were meant to survive and flourish in the thick Ozark woods, while others went home to be prepared for dinner.
 The Turkey Trot remained a hometown, word-of-mouth kind of thing over the next several decades, with pretty much every resident of Yellville and many people from the surrounding region flocking downtown once a year to eat, chitchat, and perhaps walk away with a bird. Contrary to what a few older folks told me, the event does not seem to have been the inspiration for that  famous episode of the old sitcom  WKRP in Cincinnati; hurling a bird that does not fly out of an aircraft is the kind of peculiar idea that has struck more than one great American mind, evidently.
  Things started changing when the  National Enquirer took notice. In the late 1980s, the tabloid sent two reporters to the turkey drop and ran an exposé titled “It’s Sick! Yellville Turkeys Tossed Out of Planes—For Fun.” The story described the “nightmarish scene” of the birds plunging to their death below.
 One turkey slammed into a power line so hard the wire bent down about three feet before snapping back up. The bird hit the ground, shocked and dazed, and tried to walk … pitifully trying to run on two obviously broken legs before it was crushed to death by a pileup of kids … After smashing into a tree and coming to rest on a branch, one of the birds was pursued by a gang of kids who captured and fought over it—using it in a grisly tug-of-war that ended when one boy tore the turkey’s wing off.
 (A news-of-the-weird addendum to this news-of-the-weird story: I am relying on a  secondhand version of the  Enquirer article. The publication’s archives were destroyed after  being contaminated with anthrax.)
 The prose was purple, the descriptions overwrought. But not by much. Ample documentary evidence recorded in the years since, as well as the testimonies of a number of townsfolk, indicate that this was and is pretty much what happens when you drop a turkey from hundreds of feet in the air. The panicked animals try to right themselves. Some catch a gust. Others do not. Some die when they hit the ground. Others survive with broken bones. Yet others are grievously injured when they are fought over by local kids. Some perish of apparent shock. A few, it is fair to note, are rattled, but physically unharmed.
  “I was standing in the alley behind one of the buildings in town, and that plane came right overhead,” said Rose Hilliard, who attended last year’s festival to try to save some of the birds. “We’re all trying to chase it down and I’m thinking,  Oh my God, I can’t outrun 15 kids! There’s no way. They weren’t supposed to drop them over town. They were supposed to be across the river there, across the creek. But the pilots kind of think they can do no wrong and they’re proud of it.” The turkeys she encountered were heat-stressed and in shock and bruised, she told me. “It is not entertaining to watch a frightened animal trying to get away from a crowd of people. That’s—I don’t call it entertainment.”
 But it was, in some strange and compelling way. After the  Enquirer piece, the turkey drop became not just a local-media staple but a national-media fascination. The coverage that ensued was horrified, mocking, condescending, and eye-rolling—the Turkey Trot depicted as a fall festival for the kind of people who grocery shop at the gas station, as a baroque example of human delight in animal suffering, as a can-you-believe-it bit of weird Americana.
  It was not just the media that noticed. People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals got word of the event more than 20 years ago, Daphna Nachminovitch, who leads  ’s cruelty-investigations department, told me, and has agitated against it in some way or another ever since. “When Bill Clinton was the governor of Arkansas, we started receiving complaints about it,” she told me. “Complaints from local people. We’ve been aware of it forever. It almost sounded as if somebody made it up, because who would think that it’s funny to throw a live animal out of an airplane?”
  blasted information on the event out to its followers. It petitioned the event’s organizers. It also offered $5,000 for information leading to the arrest of the pilot who drops the birds, and at one point hired a private investigator to look into things. Tommy Lee of Mötley Crüe, among others, campaigned with   to stop the drop. “Arkansas offers plenty of cool outdoor gatherings that don’t rely on sadism,” he wrote in an  open letter. “I’m writing to add my voice to the thousands of others asking you to help deep-six this sick stunt.”
 The media attention fostered activist attention, political attention, and the summoning of various local and national agencies to figure out whether there were legal means to prevent the birds-and-planes part. But animal-cruelty laws are surprisingly lax, particularly when it comes to farm animals. They are species- and purpose- specific, meaning that a scientist might be able to legally vivisect a research rat, but not her pet dog. And, of course, they need to be enforced, which they often are not. If you want cheap burgers, you need a degree of animal cruelty that most Americans would consider horrifying if confronted with it.
 Hilliard made a complaint at the local sheriff’s office. “I physically did have to do that,” she told me. “I filed a complaint for animal cruelty against the pilots of the plane. They did nothing about it. They said, ‘Well, we’d have to catch him in the act of doing it before we could even do anything.’” Ultimately, she said, “they pretty much just blew me off.”
  Animal-rights activists also looked into getting the Federal Aviation Administration involved, given that it seems unwise to let Americans fly around chucking farm animals out of planes near population centers. Last year, Lynn Lunsford, a spokesperson for the FAA, said the agency  did not have the right to intercede. “FAA regulations don’t specifically deal with dropping live animals out of airplanes, so we have no authority to prohibit the practice,” she said. Representative Dina Titus, a Nevada Democrat, attempted to spur the FAA to act, but that went nowhere either.
  As I interviewed folks about what various legal authorities were trying and failing to prevent, I marveled at how little was actually said about what needed preventing. Whose turkeys were these? Who arranged the event? Who knew what?
 Small-town omertà kicked in as I asked those questions. Yellville’s mayor would not comment for the story, a receptionist told me, because the town had nothing to do with it. “It’s a  private event put on by  private groups,” she said—never mind the fact that public schools shut down for the day to celebrate it, or that the birds got thrown off the courthouse roof, or that the town’s police would seem to have jurisdiction. Edmonds said the Chamber of Commerce had nothing to do with the turkey drop itself; in recent years, a since-identified “phantom pilot” had been the one to do the high-altitude work.
  Nevertheless, a conditional-clause, passive-voice,  no-ma’am-not-me sense of how this thing might happen emerged. The birds might have come from a backyard-type operation, not a big farm or an auction. They might have been domesticated heritage birds, not wild ones, as some Yellville residents thought. A few guys might have helped load them into an open-cage trailer behind another guy’s truck. Then it might have gone to a local airstrip, because maybe someone had a plane and thought the whole thing was a hoot. Imagine how loud and messy it would be in that tin can!
 The political and media attention on the hoot became too much. Members of the Chamber of Commerce began to receive thousands of messages, some threatening, about the event. “Local businesses were getting their servers shut down,” Edmonds told me. “They were receiving credible threats: ‘I hope your kids die a horrible, painful death, as if they were being thrown from an airplane.’” This spring, the Chamber of Commerce decided to stop sponsoring the Turkey Trot, a decision made unanimously but tearfully. The Rotary Club said it would take over as sponsor, provided that there were zero live turkeys on the premises.
 Was it overkill to focus so much attention on one town’s small, if unsavory, fall tradition? The point was never to stop the town from having a fall festival or celebrating in the way it saw fit, Nachminovitch of   said. “There was this idea that this was us thumbing our noses at them, but that’s not it,” she said. “It’s about the turkeys. It’s about animal cruelty. It’s about human decency. Where animals are being abused, that’s where we are.”
  But in town,   was cast as the villain of the Turkey Trot saga. “Congratulations, your followers’ flatulent cacophony of violent threats and extremism have killed the Turkey Trot Festival after 72 years of success,” wrote Josh Dooley, a photojournalist for   The Baxter Bulletin, a local paper. “Much like the people and practices you decry for the obliteration of various animal species, you killed something without understanding it or attempting to even comprehend the destruction you caused.”
 The festival always had strong partisans on both sides, though. A number of folks felt that it gave the town a bad image. A number were disgusted. “Let’s face it: It is just plain mean. What kind of folks are we?” read one April 2018 letter to the editor in the   Arkansas Democrat-Gazette. “If only they could speak, would turkeys not gobble out pleas to be spared the forced flight so they might be humanely join the 100 million turkeys to enjoy their God-given destiny … to have their scrawny necks severed from their unsuited-for-flight, outrageously enlarged breasted bodies” for Thanksgiving dinner.
    T  made a good point. What happens to farmed birds is far worse. That reality is well understood in this corner of Arkansas, which produces a good chunk of the birds that end up on Thanksgiving tables. The nearby town of Ozark has  been dubbed the “town that runs on turkey.” Butterball is currently  constructing a hulking plant just outside of Yellville.
  Laying hens probably take gold in the farm-animal misery Olympics. Male chicks are  threshed to death. Female chicks grow up to be hens placed in boxes so small they cannot stretch out their wings, incapable of performing any of the activities that make chickens feel like chickens. Though chickens can live for well more than a decade, laying hens are killed as soon as their egg production slows down in a year or two, if  prolapse or septicemia or cannibalism does not kill them first.
 Turkeys have it only a little better. Born in hatcheries, poults head to warehouses where they will spend the rest of their life, conditions becoming more cramped as they get bigger and bigger. Because the stress of being packed in leads them to peck one another, sometimes to death, the birds have parts of their beak and toes clipped off. Toms also have their snood—the colorful flesh that dangles near their beak—removed. Then they grow, standing in their own urine and feces, eating pellets. Many develop sores and fractures. Few receive individualized medical care. It is not unheard-of for badly injured turkeys to die of starvation.
 On the way to the slaughterhouse, they are crammed into cages, a process that frequently results in broken bones. (One million turkeys  die en route to a slaughterhouse each year, due to crushing, exposure, or another cause.) When they arrive, they are strung upside down by their feet—more broken bones—before their head is dunked in electrified water and their throat is cut. Turkeys, like chickens, are  not covered by the Humane Methods of Slaughter Act. This means there are few penalties for companies that grievously mistreat turkeys. The  production lines run so fast that birds are sometimes still alive as blades cut them, alive as they are dunked in scalding water.
  Their very bodies are no less cruel than what humans do to those bodies. Turkeys, as you might remember from elementary school, are native to the Americas. Domestication occurred hundreds or perhaps even thousands of years ago. Right around when the Yellville Turkey Trot was getting started, in the 1940s, big agricultural companies began to breed domesticated turkeys intensively to obtain more meat from them, faster. The experiment was an astonishing success: The broad-breasted white, the bird that is most likely on your Thanksgiving table, went  from a 15-pound bird in 1960 to a 30-pound bird today, and it grows to that weight far more quickly than nature intended.
 These are Franken-creatures, like bulldogs or racehorses. They cannot reproduce naturally, and their body is incompatible with a healthy, long life. The birds, if not slaughtered, face grotesque problems with their skeleton, their heart, and their respiratory system, as their cartoonishly oversized breasts distend and stress their body. “They get these compound fractures and leg injuries—often these things can’t be fixed—because they grow so fast,” said Susie Coston, the national shelter director of Farm Sanctuary, a major farm-animal rescue organization. “They are just too big.”
 Another strange thing about them: The birds suffer from compulsive insatiability. If you leave food out for them to eat at will, “they just never stop eating,” Coston told me. “I’ve never seen anything like it. They eat so much that they injure their intestinal tract.”
  If you withhold food, as is necessary to keep them alive, she said, “they are always hungry.”
   In Yellville, some birds do escape, repopulating the Ozarks as the event’s founders intended, though only briefly, given their human-cast physiology. Some are raised as pets. And some make it to farm sanctuaries. Hundreds of miles from the Ozarks sits a pocket rescue farm managed by Lisa McDonald and Joe Dinan, who also  run a brewery and evince a spirit of crunchy goodwill.
 The nonhuman residents of Sweet Bear Rescue Farm include a bristle-haired pig that occasionally presents his belly for scritches; two goats; a variety of chickens, split into two warring gangs; and a number of shaggy dogs. I visited the farm to meet a turkey that had survived Yellville. Volunteers had picked him up and helped get him and some of his brethren to Farm Sanctuary, which sent a pair to live at Sweet Bear. The turkeys were named Paul and George, after the Beatles.
 As I walked with McDonald into a paddock in front of a barn, followed Disney-princess-like by chickens, dogs, and the pig, George came running out to meet us. “He  loves people,” said McDonald, as George went on peacock-like display, the fleshy parts on his face coloring as he puffed up his iridescent feathers. McDonald pulled out a Tupperware container of tomato pieces to feed him by hand, which he pecked at berserker-like. “He loves blueberries and blackberries—those are his favorite, favorite things,” she told me.
  Now he gets to be a turkey, doing the kinds of turkey things that farmed animals rarely get
......
title: 扔不飞出飞机的鸟（2018）
title_en: Tossing a Bird That Does Not Fly Out of a Plane (2018)

note:
首先，要坦白：很长一段时间以来，我一直是Cloud所有事物的热心追随者。尽管有些人认为GCP是一个设计更好的云平台，但我对AWS服务的范围尤其印象深刻。即使您认为AWS服务的数量是混乱的界面和冲突的命名法的一团糟，您也必须承认AWS的庞大规模和经验令人难以置信。
   当我一位通过考试的同事与我分享他的Udemy课程经验时，我对证书产生了兴趣。我从星期五开始观看Udemy课程，并一直将速度提高到2倍，直到周末结束前我完成了所有200部视频。观看完视频后，我心想，那还不错，然后跳到AWS认证网站上，并在接下来的两天内为“ AWS解决方案架构师助理”考试注册了自己。注册后，我又回到了我的同事与我共享的其他2门课程，其中包括实践考试。我开始反复参加实践考试（两个课程共9个），直到我的分数接近100％。每次遇到错误的问题时，我都会针对“为什么”做笔记，以帮助我收集有关特定主题的更多信息，并在笔记中收集相关的AWS网站链接或白皮书链接并进行阅读。这比课程视频本身的帮助要大得多，课程视频本身是该主题的介绍，但是该考试测试了您在类似的AWS服务中对该主题的比较知识。例如，这是一个问题：
 公司需要将数据存储5年。该公司将需要在任何时间点都具有对数据的即时且高度可用的访问权限，但不需要频繁访问。
    C。将对象从Amazon S3标准迁移到Amazon S3一区不频繁访问（S3 One Zone-IA）
  要回答这个问题，您不仅需要了解S3中的各种存储类别，而且还需要了解它们之间的细微差异。 （如果您想知道，在这种情况下的正确答案是“ A”。）
 因此，一旦我能够掌握练习测试并仔细阅读了便笺，便可以顺利通过考试。
     如果您货比三家，可以在Udemy上获得他们的大幅折扣（最高可享受80％的折扣）
最后，如果您以前从未使用过AWS，请别忘了继续学习，并在课程学习期间动手练习。
   我在联考中获得的早期成功促使我立即进入“区域”时就注册了专业考试：)，并且将考试安排在接下来的两周内进行。一旦开始学习有关Udemy的专业课程，我就感到很头疼。讲师在开课时会警告您，这不是走蛋糕的路，这是目前最艰巨的考试之一。我有点发呆，但随后我在3天（和晚上）内振作起来，欣赏了视频系列。这次，我在视频播放时做了很多动手工作。完成课程后，我参加了一些实践考试，这使我的下一次备受关注。我什至没有在任何实践考试中获得及格分数，请记住我已经报名参加考试。我的信心动摇了……我开始意识到自己成为了邓宁-克鲁格效应的受害者。当我经历错误的问题时，它挑战了我到目前为止所学的概念。换句话说，我陷入了“绝望之谷”。
  但是我没有放弃考试，因为我已经安排好考试了，所以我继续参加并继续练习考试。我只能参加Udemy的10项实践考试，所以我不断重复这些考试。但是压力正逐渐成为我的身体表现。我有所有的典型压力症状：疼痛，溃疡，情绪波动，脚不安，食欲不振，有一天，我和朋友一起喝酒放松身心，甚至连酒都喝不下去。
  终于，考试的日子到了，我的肚子开始有了明显的恐惧，使胆汁上升。我不能强迫自己吃早餐，但设法喝了些咖啡。当我经历了头几个考试题时，我决心的余下部分就破灭了。我什么也记不清了，所以当我争先恐后地完成考试时，我的脑子只是停下来说让我们结束这件事。我没有时间复习所有答案，但完成了考试，我…失败了……我被毁了。我的妻子和女儿安慰我，接下来的两天我很沮丧，想完全放弃考试。然后，情况发生了变化：我听了2019年播客播出的一集，名为“ WOOP…！实现目标的4个步骤”。 https://www.npr.org/2016/05/10/477379965/woop-there-it-is-four-steps-to-achieve-your-goals
   我走了很长一段路，做了一些自我检查。我的障碍是速度，我需要放慢速度，并在确认之前认为答案是错误的。我知道我该怎么办。我回到了互联网上，搜寻未通过/通过专业考试的人，听过通过考试的人的YouTube视频。我了解到适用于Associate的方法不适用于Professional，因此我将对其进行纠正。我首先注册了Scott Pletcher的“ A Cloud Guru”课程，该课程得到了所有人的强烈推荐。斯科特的课程比其他课程短，但它对实际建筑和考试本身具有实际吸引力。除了视频外，Scott还提供了大量有关相关主题的白皮书和视频链接。我收集了所有材料，并从课程结束时进行的实践测试开始。这次，我开始就每个主题的差异，局限性和其他细节做笔记。与助理考试不同，专业考试有时非常冗长，问题和答案遍布整个屏幕。我意识到我需要一种新的方式来参加考试：
 尝试查看此问题是否类似于您在实践测试中遇到的问题
   提交之前，请使用问题中的高亮关键字扮演魔鬼的拥护者，以解释您的答案为何可能是错误的
联考和专业考试之间的区别在于，联考中的考试是在检查您的“什么是……”知识，但是专业考试的内容不止于此，有时答案是违反直觉的。下面的这张图简要地捕捉了大学生与专业考试的不同意图：
   一家公司当前将Amazon EBS和Amazon RDS用于存储目的。该公司打算在不同的AWS区域中使用指示灯来进行灾难恢复。该公司的RTO为6小时，RPO为24小时。
  一种。使用AWS Lambda创建每日EBS和RDS快照，并将其复制到灾难恢复区域。将Amazon Route 53与主动-被动故障转移配置一起使用。在灾难恢复区域的容量设置为0的Auto Scaling组中使用Amazon EC2。
 b。使用AWS Lambda创建每日EBS和RDS快照，并将其复制到灾难恢复区域。将Amazon Route 53与主动-主动故障转移配置一起使用。在以与主要区域相同的方式配置的Auto Scaling组中使用Amazon EC2。
 c。使用Amazon ECS处理长期运行的任务，以创建每日EBS和RDS快照，并将其复制到灾难恢复区域。将Amazon Route 53与主动-被动故障转移配置一起使用。在灾难恢复区域的容量设置为0的Auto Scaling组中使用Amazon EC2。
 d。使用EBS和RDS跨区域快照复制功能在灾难恢复区域中创建快照。将Amazon Route 53与主动-主动故障转移配置一起使用。在灾难恢复区域的容量设置为0的Auto Scaling组中使用Amazon EC2。
  我收集了到目前为止参加的所有实践考试（其中Udemy，aCloudGuru和Internet上大约进行了12项考试），总计约1200个问题（显然有些重复）。我遇到了一个很棒的网站，名为pegacert，提供大约560个练习题，而最好的部分是，不同于练习考试不会立即为您提供答案的反馈，Pegacert会立即告诉您是/否。我非常喜欢这种方法，因此着手编写一个小程序以将我的练习问题加载到数据库中，以备不时之需。我的女儿走进我的办公室，问我在做什么，我向她解释了我在做什么，她说：“您不必这样做，我可以帮助您。”她在聊天中扮演我的督导员，将问题粘贴到聊天中，然后我会回答，她会回答我/是。
失败14天后，AWS不允许您重新安排考试时间。因此，当重新安排日期到来时，我为自己预定了大约10天的另一门考试。这次，我选择了30分钟的额外津贴（这适用于ESL人士）现在，我加倍努力，浏览了视频，阅读了白皮书，观看了一些AWS re：Invent视频。更重要的是，练习考试的问题和解释是我的救恩，但是，我遇到了另一个障碍。在实践考试中，重复了一些问题，但答案/解释不匹配。现在，我不得不查看每个冗长的问题，并试图找到正确的答案：我找到了examtopics.com，可让您讨论来自AWS考试的真实问题并讨论答案。这打开了另一个Pandora的盒子，因为那里没有人在任何答案上都同意另一个人，而且他们都有关于原因的有效白皮书/论据。这在我心中播下了怀疑的种子，我开始再次质疑自己的准备情况。最后，我想相信Pegacert的回答并坚持自己的理智。在我的女儿和Pegacert的帮助下，我漂洗了一下，并在接下来的2周中重复了问题/答案会议。
 考试那天，我醒来，强迫自己吃早餐，冥想并做一些练习考试。考试前，我花时间看了一些喜剧情景喜剧（布鲁克林九岁家庭喜剧），以放松身心。我没有告诉任何人我正在参加真正的考试，我告诉妻子我正在进行模拟考试（;），因为我告诉自己最糟糕的是什么？ （回忆一下WOOP）尽管我的收入降低了$ 300，但我现在仍将是我的所在地。因此，考试继续进行，当我回答前几个问题时，我变得更加放松，并且能够控制不断增加的身体恐惧感。我花了时间，标记了我不确定的问题，并有时间在时钟用完之前修改所有问题，当我单击“期末考试”时，我告诉自己，没有任何问题，然后……。通过！！！ …有趣的是，尽管练习题有相似之处，但我无法完全匹配90％的实际题来练习题，但是，如果没有这些练习考试，我就不会在这里。
        Examtopics.com [此网站上的搜索功能无用，您实际上可以通过在Google上用问题文本进行搜索并单击Examtopics的结果来到达确切的问题/讨论页面）
 因此，我花了1个月的时间从没有AWS认证到获得2个AWS认证。不知道您可以从中学到什么，但是如果他们需要答案正确与错误的指示，我将非常乐意与任何人讨论任何考试主题。我鼓励您从自己的练习考试中复制/粘贴，而不是让我分享我的笔记或问题，这将使您专注于需要重点关注的领域而不是我的主题。
 PS：撰写本文2周后，我通过了另一项AWS认证：使用相同方法的“ AWS认证机器学习专业”。
note_en:
First, a confession: I’ve been an ardent follower of all things Cloud for a long time now. I am especially impressed by the range of AWS services although according to some, GCP is a better designed cloud platform. Even if you think the number of AWS services is a hot mess of confusing interfaces and conflicting nomenclature, you gotta admit that the sheer scale and experience of AWS is incredulous.
   I got interested in certificat  ion when one of my colleagues who passed the exam shared his Udemy course experience with me. I started watching the Udemy courses on a Friday and kept increasing the speed to 2X until I finished all the 200 videos by the time the weekend was over. Once I finished with the videos, I thought to myself, that wasn’t so bad and jumped onto the AWS certification website and registered myself for the “AWS Solution Architect Associate” exam in the next 2 days. Once I registered, I went back to 2 other courses my colleague shared with me that contained practice exams. I started taking the practice exams (total of 9 across both courses) repeatedly until I was scoring close to 100%. Every time I got a question wrong, I made notes on the “why” that helped me gather additional info on a particular topic and in my notes, I gathered the relevant AWS website link or whitepaper link and read it. This helped much more than the course videos themselves which act as an intro to the topic but the exam tests your comparative knowledge on a topic across similar AWS services. For example, here’s a question:
 A company needs to store data for 5 years. The company will need to have immediate and highly available access to the data at any point in time, but will not require frequent access.
    c. Transition objects from Amazon S3 Standard to Amazon S3 One Zone-Infrequent Access (S3 One Zone-IA)
  To answer this question, you not only need to know about the various storage classes in S3 but subtle differences between them when compared to each other. (The correct answer in this case “A” if you are wondering.)
 So, once I was able to master the practice tests and went over my ad-hoc notes, I was able to successfully pass the exam.
     You can get them a steep discount on Udemy if you shop around (up to 80% off)
  Lastly, if you haven’t worked with AWS before, don’t forget to follow along and get your hands dirty with the lab exercises during the course.
   My early success with the associate exam prompted me to register for the Professional exam right away while I am in the “zone” as it were :) and I scheduled my exam to a date in the next 2 weeks. Once I started with the Professional course on Udemy, I got my first jolt. The instructor opens the course with a warning that this is not a cake walk and it’s one of the toughest exams out there. I was fazed a bit but then I picked myself up and breezed through the video series in 3 days (and nights). This time, I did more hands-on work to follow along while the videos were playing. Once I finished the courses, I took some of the practice exams which held my next jolt in reserve. I didn’t even score passing grades in any of the practice exams, keep in mind that I already registered for the exam. My confidence was shaken… I was starting to realize that I became the victim of  Dunning-Kruger effect. As I went through the questions I got wrong, it challenged the very notion of what I learnt so far. In other words, I fell into the ‘valley of despair’.
  But I haven’t given up on the exam because I already scheduled it, so I pressed on and kept taking the practice exams. I had access to only 10 practice exams from Udemy so I kept repeating them. But the stress was catching up to me as a physical manifestation. I had all the classic symptoms of stress: aches, ulcers, mood swings, restless foot, crazy appetites, and one day I had drinks with friends to relax and couldn’t even hold the liquor down.
  Finally, the day of exam arrived and I started the day with palpable dread in my stomach that rising up my bile. I couldn’t force myself to eat any breakfast but managed to drink some coffee. As I went through first few of the exam questions, the remnants of my resolve shattered. I couldn’t remember anything so while I was scrambling to finish the exam, my mind was just shutting down to say let’s just get this over with. I didn’t have time to review all the answers but finished the exam and I … FAILED … I was devastated. My wife and daughter consoled me and for the next 2 days I was down and wanted to give up on the exam altogether. Then, something changed: I listened to a Podcast episode from 2019 called “WOOP… There It is! 4 Steps to Achieve Your Goals”.  https://www.npr.org/2016/05/10/477379965/woop-there-it-is-four-steps-to-achieve-your-goals
   I went for a long walk and did some self-examination. My obstacle was speed, I needed to slow down and consider my answers are wrong before confirming. I knew what I had to do. I went back to the Internet and searched up on people who failed/passed the Professional exam, listened to YouTube videos of people who passed the exam. I understood that the approach that worked for Associate won’t work for Professional and I set about to correct it. I started by signing up for A Cloud Guru course by Scott Pletcher that was highly recommended by everyone. Scott’s course is shorter than other courses but it had a practical appeal to it that’s geared towards real architecture and of course the exam itself. Along with the videos, Scott provides a plethora of whitepapers and video links on relevant topics. I gathered all the material and started with the practice tests given at the end of the course. This time, I started taking notes on each topic on the differences, limitations and other minutiae. Unlike the Associate, the Professional exam is very verbose sometime the questions and answers fill the entire screen. I realized I needed a new approach to take the exam:
 Try to see if this question is similar to something you encountered in a practice test
   Before submitting, play the devil’s advocate on why your answer might be wrong using the highlight keywords in the question
 The difference between the Associate and Professional exam is that in the Associate exam, the test is checking your ‘what is…’ knowledge, but the professional exam goes beyond that, sometimes the answers are counter-intuitive. This image below succinctly captures the different intentions of Associate Vs Professional exams:
   A company currently uses Amazon EBS and Amazon RDS for storage purposes. The company intends to use a pilot light approach for disaster recovery in a different AWS Region. The company has an RTO of 6 hours and an RPO of 24 hours.
  a. Use AWS Lambda to create daily EBS and RDS snapshots, and copy them to the disaster recovery region. Use Amazon Route 53 with active-passive failover configuration. Use Amazon EC2 in an Auto Scaling group with the capacity set to 0 in the disaster recovery region.
 b. Use AWS Lambda to create daily EBS and RDS snapshots, and copy them to the disaster recovery region. Use Amazon Route 53 with active-active failover configuration. Use Amazon EC2 in an Auto Scaling group configured in the same way as in the primary region.
 c.Use Amazon ECS to handle long-running tasks to create daily EBS and RDS snapshots, and copy to the disaster recovery region. Use Amazon Route 53 with active-passive failover configuration. Use Amazon EC2 in an Auto Scaling group with the capacity set to 0 in the disaster recovery region.
 d. Use EBS and RDS cross-region snapshot copy capability to create snapshots in the disaster recovery region. Use Amazon Route 53 with active-active failover configuration. Use Amazon EC2 in an Auto Scaling group with the capacity set to 0 in the disaster recovery region.
  I gathered all the practice exams that I took so far (around 12 of them across Udemy and aCloudGuru and Internet) that totals to around 1200 questions (with some repetitions obviously). I encountered a great website called pegacert that offers around 560 practice questions and the best part is, unlike a practice exam which doesn’t give you feedback on your answer right away, Pegacert tells you Yes/No instantly. I liked that approach so much I set about writing a small program to load my practice questions into a database to be by instant advocate. My daughter walked in to my office and asked me what I was doing I explained to her what I was working on, she said “you don’t have to do that, I can help you.” She played my proctor on chat where she would paste a question into the chat and I would answer and she would give me yes/no, if I got a question wrong, she would pin the question and give me explanation.
 AWS doesn’t let you reschedule an exam after you fail for 14 days. So, when the rescheduling date rolled in, I booked myself for another exam set in about 10 days. This time, I opted for the extra 30 minutes allowance (this is for people with ESL) Now, I redoubled all my efforts, I went through the videos, read the whitepapers, watched some AWS re:Invent videos. More than all this, practice exam questions with explanations were my salvation, however, I hit another snag. Across the practice exams, some questions were repeated but the answers/explanations were not matching. Now, I had to look at each of the verbose questions and tried to find the right answer: I found examtopics.com that lets you discuss real questions from AWS exams and discuss answers. This opened up another Pandora’s box because not one person there agrees with another on any answer and they all have valid whitepapers/arguments as to why. This sowed seeds of doubt in my mind and I started questioning my readiness again. In the end, I wanted to trust Pegacert’s answers and hang on to my sanity. With the help of my daughter and Pegacert, I rinsed and repeated the question/answer sessions for the next 2 weeks.
 On the day of the exam, I woke up, forced myself to eat breakfast, do some meditation and did some practice exams. Before the exam, spent my time watching some comedy sit-coms (Brooklyn Nine Nine is a family favorite) to relax. I didn’t tell anyone that I am taking the exam for real, I told my wife that I am doing a simulated exam (;)) because I told myself whats the worst? (Recall WOOP) I will be exactly where I am right now albeit $300 poorer. So, the exam rolled on, and as I addressed the first few questions, I was much more relaxed and was able to keep the physical dread that’s rising in me in check. I took my time, marked the questions I wasn’t sure of and had time to revise all the questions before the clock ran out and as I clicked on ‘End Exam’ I told myself, nothing matters, and I ….  PASSED!!! … The funny thing is, though there are similarities to practice questions, I couldn’t exactly match 90% of the actual questions to practice questions, nonetheless, without those practice exams, I wouldn’t be here.
        Examtopics.com [The search function on this site is useless, you can actually reach the exact question/discussion page by searching with question text on Google and clicking on results from Examtopics)
 So, it took me 1 month from no AWS certification to 2 AWS certifications. Not sure what you can learn from this, but I would be more than willing to discuss any exam topic with anyone if they need pointers on why an answer is right/wrong. Instead of asking me to share my notes or questions, I encourage you to copy/paste from the practice exams you self that will let you focus on areas you need to focus on rather than my topics.
 PS: After 2 weeks of writing this, I passed another AWS certification: “AWS Certified Machine Learning Specialty” using the same methodology.

“维修权”呼吁制造商让消费者更轻松地修理自己的设备，并更加透明地了解其产品的使用寿命。
  欧洲议会已投票支持欧洲的“维修权”规则，该规则将使消费者更容易修理自己的设备，同时还严厉打击制造商缩短其产品寿命的做法。
    今年早些时候，欧盟委员会宣布了计划在2021年制定涵盖智能手机，平板电脑和笔记本电脑的新的“维修权”规则，作为应对电子废物并帮助欧洲在2050年实现气候中和的道路上做出更大努力的一部分。
  该提案旨在通过扩大制造商的担保，为更换的零件提供担保或提供对设备维修和维护信息的更好访问，来使维修更具吸引力，并使消费者更容易获得维修。
  11月25日，星期三，欧洲议会以压倒性多数投票通过了395票赞成，94票反对的关于更可持续单一市场的决议。
   现在，欧洲委员被要求为在商店和在线销售的产品开发和引入标签系统，以帮助他们了解产品的可维修性。
 还敦促欧盟禁止硬件制造商的做法，“其目的是故意缩短产品的寿命，例如在设计阶段防止维修或在软件更新后导致性能下降”（也称为计划过时）。
提案中也将限制设备制造商的“绿色清洗”做法（一种营销策略，通过该策略品牌暗示其环保或可持续发展，而没有实际提供任何证据来支持其主张）。
 欧洲议会法国议员戴维·科曼德（David Cormand）表示：“通过这份报告，欧洲议会发出了一个明确的信息：统一的强制性标签表明耐用性和解决欧盟一级的过时淘汰问题是前进的道路。”
  提案的含义（如果通过）是多种多样的。可以鼓励制造商大幅延长其设备的保修期限，或者重新考虑他们用来延长产品寿命的材料。对于消费者而言，“维修权”规则可以使他们更容易按照OEM提供的说明更换笔记本电脑电池，SSD或在家中自行展示。
 另外，这些规则可以使消费者有更多的自由，可以使用更便宜的独立服务来对其设备进行维修，而不必依赖OEM-设备制造商不太热衷这种做法，通常意味着消费者会放弃他们购买的保修。
 根据欧洲晴雨表（Eurobarometer）的一项调查，有77％的欧盟公民宁愿修理其设备而不是更换它们，而79％的人认为制造商应负有法律义务促进数字设备的修理或单个零件的更换。
 在法国，自2021年1月起，将针对智能手机，笔记本电脑，洗衣机，电视和割草机引入可修复性等级。预计该等级将扩大到更多类别的产品。
 欧盟法律的任何变更当然都需要时间，而且目前很难确切预测制造商将如何影响新规则。即使这样，投票还是对消费者来说是一个巨大的胜利，并且将给制造商施加压力，要求他们开始对其产品的可修复性和耐用性更加透明。
iFixit欧洲公司宣传总监Matthias Huisken表示：“这对整个欧洲的消费者来说都是一个巨大的胜利。这次投票将启动一系列新的友好维修政策，从零售维修分数到产品使用寿命的披露。”

'Right to repair' calls on manufacturers to make it easier for consumers to fix their own devices and to be more transparent about the lifespan of their products.
  The European Parliament has voted in favor of "right to repair" rules for Europe that would make it easier for consumers to repair their own devices, while also cracking down on practices used by manufacturers to shorten the lifespan of their products.
    Earlier this year the European Commission announced plans for new "right to repair" rules covering smartphones, tablets and laptops in 2021, as part of wider efforts to tackle e-waste and help Europe on its path to achieve climate-neutrality by 2050.
  The proposal seeks to make repairs more appealing and easier to access by consumers, either by extending guarantees from manufacturers, providing guarantees for replaced parts, or by providing better access to information on device repair and maintenance.
  On Wednesday 25 November, the European Parliament voted overwhelmingly in support of the proposal, with the resolution on a more sustainable single market adopted with 395 in favor and 94 opposed.
   European Commissioners are now being called on to develop and introduce a labelling system for products sold in stores and online to help them understand how repairable a product is.
 The EU is also being urged to ban practices by hardware makers "aimed at intentionally shortening the lifetime of a product, such as preventing repair at the design stage or causing a slowdown in performance after a software update" – otherwise known as planned obsolescence.
 The practice of "greenwashing" by device makers – a marketing tactic through which brands imply that that are eco-friendly or sustainable without actually providing any evidence to back up their claims - would also be curbed under the proposals.
 French Member of European Parliament (MEP), David Cormand,  said: "By adopting this report, the European Parliament sent a clear message: harmonized mandatory labelling indicating durability and tackling premature obsolescence at EU level are the way forward."
  Implications of the proposals – if passed into law – are manifold. Manufacturers could be encouraged to considerably extend warranties on their devices, or reconsider the materials they use to extend the lifespan of their products. For consumers, "right to repair" rules could make it easier for them to replace, say, a laptop battery, SSD or display themselves at home, using instructions provided by the OEM.
 Alternatively, the rules could give consumers more freedom to use cheaper, independent services to carry out repairs on their devices, rather than having to rely on the OEM – a practice that device makers aren't too keen on, and often means consumers voiding the warranty on their purchase.
 According to  a Eurobarometer survey, 77% of EU citizens would rather repair their devices than replace them, while 79% think that manufacturers should be legally obliged to facilitate the repair of digital devices or the replacement of their individual parts.
 In France,  reparability ratings will be introduced for smartphones, laptops, washing machines, TVs and lawn mowers starting in January 2021. This is expected to be expanded to more categories of products.
 Any changes to EU legislation will, of course, take time, and it's hard to predict at this point exactly how manufacturers will be impacted the new rules. Even so, the vote is a big win for consumers, and will put pressure on manufacturers to start being more transparent about the reparability and the durability of their products.
 Matthias Huisken, director of advocacy for iFixit Europe,  said: "This is a huge win for consumers across Europe. This vote will set in motion a wave of new repair-friendly policies, from repair scores at retail to product longevity disclosures."

不久前，急诊室护士珍·桑多瓦尔（Jane Sandoval）与丈夫坐在一起，看着她最喜欢的NFL球队，旧金山49人队。她每隔一个星期日休息一次，即使在冠状病毒大流行期间，这也是一种仪式。 Jane和Carlos看着，欢呼，大喊–只是一对夫妇的逃生方法。
        然而，对于桑多瓦尔来说，随着季节（以及大流行）的持续恶化，享受它变得越来越困难。在赛季初，49ers队的Kyle Shanahan是违反联盟要求所有场边人员戴面罩的5名教练之一。简注意到，即使在加利福尼亚州和整个美国，冠状病毒病例再次激增，李维斯体育场也在考虑允许球迷观看比赛。
   桑多瓦尔说，但是最难忽视的是，就冠状病毒检测而言，这是一个有与没有的国家。
  在专业人士和大学运动员当中，尤其是踢足球的运动员。从11月8日至14日，NFL对7,856名球员，教练和员工进行了43,148项测试。大型大学橄榄球课程每天提供数十种测试，试图保持健康并防止计划中断，这是徒劳的。上周，美国职业足球大联盟（Major League Soccer）进行了近5,000项测试，而棒球职业大联盟（Major League Baseball）在被淘汰的赛季中进行了约170,000项测试。
 同时，桑多瓦尔（Sandoval）是一位58岁的前线工作者，他定期治疗怀疑或证实被冠状病毒感染的患者。在八个月中，她从未接受过测试。她说，她的雇主加利福尼亚太平洋医疗中心即使在可能暴露后也拒绝为其医务人员提供测试。
 因此，观看运动不再代表Sandoval脱离现实。她说，取而代之的是，这表明了国家优先考虑的事情。
 她谈到冠状病毒测试时说：“体育界的供应无穷。” “您正在举起手臂。我和下一个人一样喜欢运动。但是，谁在接受测试与谁没有接受测试之间存在差异，这没有任何意义。”
本月，注册护士聚集在洛杉矶，以抗议加州大学洛杉矶分校的体育部门在一周内进行了1,248项测试，而加州大学洛杉矶分校医院的医护人员被拒绝进行测试。上周，美国最大的护士工会国民护士联合会（National Nurses United）发布了一项针对15,000多名成员的调查结果。大约三分之二的人报告说他们从未接受过测试。
借助我们的免费冠状病毒新闻通讯，赶上流行病的最大发展。自8月份NFL训练营开幕以来，美国最受欢迎，实力最强的体育联盟-年收入超过150亿美元-进行了大约645,000次冠状病毒测试。
 加州另一名注册护士兼体育迷米歇尔·古铁雷斯·沃（Michelle Gutierrez Vo）说：“这些运动员和球队拥有足够的共性测试，足以随意测试。” “看起来很痛苦。似乎其他人都没有关系，或者他们的生活比我们的生活更重要。”
 大流行已经数月，并且疫苗也即将分发，因此在美国进行检测仍然是一种奢侈。测试现场人满为患，有些患者仍然报告等待结果的时间。桑多瓦尔说，怀疑自己被暴露在外的护士应自费寻找测试地点，费用由他们自己承担，并在等待结果时花费无偿时间-实际上是在薪水和健康以及其他人的健康之间进行选择。
 亚利桑那州立大学生物医学诊断学教授玛拉·阿斯皮纳尔（Mara Aspinall）表示：“当前（总统）行政管理部门并不专注于测试，而是专注于疫苗。 “我们应该将相同的“变形速度”集中在测试上。我们还会需要疫苗吗？是的，但是我们将在此过程中挽救更多生命，并使人们更有信心去上班。”
 在大流行的爆发浪潮中，四个月的停工后，职业运动于七月回归。在最不寻常的一年中，这不仅仅是电视比赛，它还象征着舒适和例行公事。但是，随着运动日历的发展以及做出的重大调整，几乎无法忽略一切外观，声音和感觉如何不同。
 体育场是空的，或几乎是空的，而某些运动却带有气泡，而另一些则假装其球形是不可渗透的。教练站在边线旁，上面戴着模糊的面罩；名册和时间表不断改组。周六，克莱姆森和佛罗里达州立大学之间的大学橄榄球比赛在开球前三个小时被取消。道奇体育场（Dodger Stadium）是世界大赛冠军的故乡，是一个巨大的测试场地，一排排的汽车在停车场内蜿蜒而行。
换句话说，体育并不是对两极分化的国家及其对全球流行病的反应的干扰。他们不断提醒着他们。而且，当一些护士转向运动以逃脱时，这只是另一个图像，表明谁应该优先进行测试，而通常谁没有。
 “当您现在观看体育比赛时，会断开连接。这是不一样的。 Covid改变了一切，”在加利福尼亚州弗里蒙特（Kaiser Permanente）的Kaiser Permanente工作的古铁雷斯·沃（Gutierrez Vo）说，“我试着不去考虑。”
 桑多瓦尔（Sandoval）尝试做同样的事情，告诉自己看比赛是让自己再次像2月的事情。当时，冠状病毒是一种遥远的威胁，而49人队则在超级碗中。
 那天晚上，桑多瓦尔的急诊室发生了变化，在病人之间，她会躲进休息室或挤在同一个同事旁边，在电话里检查分数。 49人队曾担任堪萨斯城酋长队的球员，桑多瓦尔回忆起她最喜欢的球队以两位数的领先优势代表了当天最大的压力。
 现在，在轮班期间，桑多瓦尔有时会与那些坚持认为感染他们的病毒是一种媒体驱动的骗局的患者争论。即使没有确诊患者是否患有冠状病毒，她也要掩盖面罩并戴上口罩，尽管她无法自拔。
 “我洗手了吗？我碰了眼镜吗？我要特别小心吗？”她说。
 她说，如果桑多瓦尔（Sandoval）怀疑自己已被暴露，则不必费心要求进行检查。她说医院会说还不够。因此，她改为自我监控并补充维生素C和锌，希望她的喉咙发痒消失。如果症状持续存在（她说尚未发生），她计划自己找到一个测试站点。但这意味着要花无偿的时间，自掏腰包支付费用，呆在家里-并没收薪水-直到结果到来。
国民护士联合会说，一些护士被告知要报告工作，因为他们等待可能需要三到五天的结果。负责加利福尼亚太平洋医疗中心的医院系统Sutter Health在给《华盛顿邮报》的一份声明中说，该系统为被视为高风险的员工以及任何出现症状的员工提供检查。该声明称，有症状的员工在等待测试结果时享有带薪休假。
 Sutter的声明中写道：“只要一名基本的医护人员没有症状，他们就可以在等待测试结果的同时继续工作并进行自我监控。”
 桑多瓦尔说，员工已经被告知医院的员工健康部门将与任何有接触的人联系。尽管她认为自己在每个轮班中都会暴露在外，但桑多瓦尔说，员工的健康状况从未与她联系过以提供测试或进行联系人追踪。
 她说：“如果您觉得需要测试，可以自己做。”桑多瓦尔怀疑这种失衡是经济上的。 9月，《福布斯》（Forbes）报告说，尽管有大流行，但NFL球队的收入却增长了7％。上周，萨特健康公司（Sutter Health）报告称，到2020年的前9个月，该公司亏损了6.07亿美元。
 桑多瓦尔（Sandoval）尽量避免考虑这一点，因此她不断回去工作，并希望自己做到最好。尽管她说对运动的热情现在已经降低了，但是她仍然喜欢在患者戴上团队标志时谈论运动。她询问明星球员或最近的比赛。她说，她很期待49ers队的下一场比赛和2021年棒球季。
 桑多瓦尔说，有时候，患者会询问她的工作以及她避免感染冠状病毒的方式。桑多瓦尔说，患者总是说，她每天必须接受最多的检查。
 她只是翻了个白眼，轻笑着。她说，那只有在你是运动员的情况下才会发生。
